3: YHWH erech apayim ugedol koach venakeh lo yenakeh YHWH besufa uvishara darko veanan afar raglav: 'The LORD is slow to anger and great in power, and the LORD will by no means clear the guilty. His way is in whirlwind and storm, and the clouds are the dust of his feet.' Nahum opens with an acrostic poem (Nahum 1:2-8, partial alphabet) on the character of YHWH — combining the divine attributes of mercy and judgment that Exodus 34:6-7 announced: merciful and slow to anger (Exodus 34:6; Nahum 1:3a) but will not leave the guilty unpunished (Exodus 34:7b; Nahum 1:3a). Jonah announced repentance and YHWH relented; Nahum announces the final judgment because Nineveh did not permanently repent. The same divine character — patient mercy but ultimate justice — is the framework for the NT: God overlooked past sins (Acts 17:30; Romans 3:25) but now commands repentance in light of the coming judgment.
1: massa Nineveh, sefer chazot Nachum ha-Elqoshi — "The burden of Nineveh; the book of the vision of Nahum the Elkoshite." Massa (burden, oracle) is the standard heading for oracles of judgment — what is lifted up and carried. It is the word of the message and the weight of judgment simultaneously. Sefer chazot (book of the vision) is unusual — most prophetic books are not called "books" within themselves. Nahum identifies what follows as a written, documented vision, not an oral proclamation. Nineveh, the capital of Assyria, is the explicit target from the first word.
2: El qanna unoqem YHWH — "A jealous and avenging God is YHWH." The three-fold noqem (avenger) in Nahum 1:2-3 establishes the character of the God who will act against Nineveh: jealous (qanna, the covenant-possessiveness that will not share his people with any rival), avenging, and lord of wrath (ba'al chemah). This is not emotional volatility but covenantal justice — the same character that in Exodus 34:6-7 is simultaneously "abounding in steadfast love" and "will by no means leave the guilty unpunished." Nahum picks up the second half of that formula and unpacks it against Nineveh.
4: go'er ba-yam va-yavshehu — "He rebukes the sea and makes it dry." The theophanic display of divine power over creation: the sea dried at YHWH's rebuke (ga'ar), Bashan and Carmel wither, the flower of Lebanon fades. These are the most fertile and productive regions of Israel's world — YHWH's rebuke reaches the most verdant places. The sea-drying invokes the Exodus (Exod 14) and the Jordan-crossing (Josh 3) — YHWH's power to dry the sea is established history, not mythology.
5: harim ra'ashu mimenu — "The mountains quake before him." The cosmic shaking continues: hills melt, the earth lifts up (or is heaved up), the world and all its inhabitants. The language is theophanic (compare Sinai in Exod 19; Ps 68; Hab 3) — the arrival of YHWH produces cosmic disruption. Against this backdrop, Nineveh's formidable military power is relativized: what chance does the empire have against the one at whose footfall mountains tremble?
6: lifnei za'amo mi ya'amod — "Who can stand before his indignation?" The rhetorical question expects the answer "no one." Za'am (indignation, fierce displeasure — stronger than anger) and charon apo (burning of his anger) describe intensity; nitzkah chemato ka-esh (his wrath poured out like fire) describes the manner. The rocks are shattered before him — not gradually but in the moment of theophanic arrival. Nahum answers Nineveh's implicit taunt ("who can stand against us?") with the counter-question that the only One who matters has already answered.
7: tov YHWH le-ma'oz be-yom tzara — "YHWH is good, a stronghold (ma'oz) in the day of trouble." The pivot within the theophany: the same God whose wrath melts mountains is simultaneously a refuge for those who trust him. Chasay bo (those who take refuge in him) identifies the category of the protected — those who have entered into covenantal shelter. This is the theological center of the book: YHWH's power that destroys his enemies is the same power that protects his people. One attribute does not limit the other; both flow from the same holiness.
8: uveshitfah over kalah ya'aseh meqomah — "He will make a complete end of its place with an overflowing flood." The flood (shitfah over) is the instrument of Nineveh's end — historically, the city's walls were breached when the Tigris flooded and weakened them (2:6 will develop this). "Darkness (choshekh) pursues his enemies" — the darkness that YHWH deploys against enemies in Exodus (plague of darkness) becomes the instrument of pursuit. The end is total (kalah = completion, annihilation).
9: ma techa'shevun el-YHWH — "What do you plot against YHWH?" The oracle turns to address the one plotting against YHWH (Sennacherib? The Assyrian empire generally?). The assurance: "He will make a complete end; trouble will not rise up a second time." Nineveh will fall once and never recover — the judgment will not need to be repeated because it will be comprehensive. The question "What do you plot?" underscores the futility of strategizing against YHWH.
10: ki ad sirim sevukhim — "For like entangled thorns they are drunk; they are consumed like fully dry stubble." The similes depict both vulnerability and flammability: entangled thorns cannot move and are easily burned; drunk men cannot fight. The military might of Assyria, which seemed impenetrable, is reframed as combustible material awaiting the spark. The image of interlocking thorns may also suggest internal chaos — the empire entangling itself.
11: mimech yatza choshev al-YHWH ra'ah — "From you has gone out one who plots evil against YHWH, a worthless counselor (yo'etz beliya'al)." The direct address to Nineveh: from this city came the one who counseled wickedness against YHWH. The identity is probably Sennacherib, whose campaign against Jerusalem (701 BCE) was experienced as an assault on YHWH himself (cf. Isa 37). The counselor of worthlessness is the antithesis of the divine Counselor (Yo'etz) of Isaiah 9.
12: koh amar YHWH im shlemim — "Thus says YHWH: Though they are at full strength and many, even so they will be cut off and pass away." The oracle to Judah pivots: though I have afflicted you (inniticha), I will afflict you no more. The Assyrian affliction of Judah was within YHWH's purposes; but it is now over. The same YHWH who used Assyria as his rod (Isaiah 10:5) now snaps the rod.
13: ve-ata eshbor motehu me-alayikh — "And now I will break his yoke from off you and burst your bonds apart." The ol (yoke) and moser (bonds) are the standard images of imperial subjugation. The breaking of the yoke by YHWH rather than by military resistance is the key: Judah's liberation is not self-achieved. The same language recurs in Jeremiah and Isaiah for the messianic liberation.
14: ve-tziva alecha YHWH lo yizara mish-shimkha od — "YHWH has given commandment about you: your name shall be perpetuated no more." The command against the Assyrian king (or Nineveh): the divine name (shem) will be cut off. The temple idols (pesel and massekah, carved image and cast image) will be cut off from their place. The king of Assyria who claimed divine descent and whose name was the axis of imperial ideology will have his name erased. "I will make your grave, for you are vile (taqal)."
15: hineh al-heharim raglei mevasser — "Behold, upon the mountains, the feet of him who brings good tidings (mevasser)." The herald on the mountains proclaiming peace and the good news of Judah's salvation is the exact image Isaiah 52:7 uses for the servant's proclamation: mah navu al-heharim raglei mevasser (how beautiful on the mountains are the feet of him who brings good tidings). Both texts use mevasser; both announce shalom. Nahum's herald announces Nineveh's fall as Judah's liberation; Isaiah's herald announces the eschatological liberation from exile. The call to Judah: "Celebrate your feasts, O Judah; fulfill your vows — for the worthless one shall no more pass through you; he is utterly cut off."
